title: Shared prosperity constitutes both income growth and inequality reduction
gist: World Bank researchers introduce the Prosperity Gap, a measure based on multiplying incomes up to a $28/person/day standard, arguing it beats the Gini index and bottom-40 income growth because it captures both income level and its distribution, and show the global gap has halved since 1981 (13 to 4.5) while Sub-Saharan Africa remains roughly 40 years behind.

insights.claim: The Gini index measures how income is distributed within an economy but says nothing about the level of welfare, so two countries can have identical Gini scores while one is far richer than the other.
insights.speaker: Maarten Lambrechts, Jonas Helth Lønborg, and Nishant Yonzan
insights.so_what: Warns against using inequality metrics alone as a development or country-risk signal without pairing them with an income-level measure.
insights.quote: As such, most would argue that shared prosperity is greater in the richer country.
insights.locator: The Gini index captures how welfare is shared

insights.claim: By the World Bank's own definition of high inequality (Gini index above 40), the number of economies classified as highly unequal has declined by more than a third over the past three decades.
insights.speaker: Maarten Lambrechts, Jonas Helth Lønborg, and Nishant Yonzan
insights.so_what: A concrete, trackable base rate for how many economies remain in the high-inequality bucket, useful as a screening filter for EM inequality risk.
insights.quote: the number of economies with high inequality has declined by more than a third in the past three decades.
insights.locator: The World Bank reports the number of economies

insights.claim: The widely used bottom-40 income-growth measure (comparing income growth of the poorest 40% to the average) ignores the absolute income level and, within the bottom 40%, systematically overweights its richer members relative to its poorest members.
insights.speaker: Maarten Lambrechts, Jonas Helth Lønborg, and Nishant Yonzan
insights.so_what: Flags a structural blind spot in a metric widely cited in shared-prosperity reporting — it can look favorable even while the very poorest are lagging.
insights.quote: the bottom 40 measure gives more importance to the richer individuals within the bottom 40 percent than the poorer individuals that we really care about.
insights.locator: Another well-used measure to track shared progress

insights.claim: The World Bank's Prosperity Gap measure is defined as the average factor by which individuals' incomes must be multiplied to reach a global prosperity standard set at $28 per person per day, combining both income level and its distribution into a single number.
insights.speaker: Maarten Lambrechts, Jonas Helth Lønborg, and Nishant Yonzan
insights.so_what: A new composite metric investors and policy analysts may see referenced going forward as the World Bank's preferred shared-prosperity headline number.
insights.quote: the average factor by which individuals’ incomes must be multiplied to reach a global prosperity standard, currently set at $28 per person per day.
insights.locator: The Prosperity Gap, a measure developed

insights.claim: The Prosperity Gap is implicitly progressive: an individual earning $14/day needs to double their income to hit the $28/day standard (gap of 2), while one earning $7/day needs to quadruple it (gap of 4), so the poorer person counts for twice the weight in the aggregate measure.
insights.speaker: Maarten Lambrechts, Jonas Helth Lønborg, and Nishant Yonzan
insights.so_what: Explains the mechanism by which the metric avoids the bottom-40 measure's flaw of underweighting the very poorest.
insights.quote: this measure gives the person with half the income double the importance.
insights.locator: Consider an individual earning $14 a day

insights.claim: The Global Prosperity Gap has more than halved since 1981, falling from close to 13 to 4.5 in 2026, meaning average world incomes needed to rise almost 13-fold in 1981 versus only 4.5-fold today to reach the $28/day threshold for everyone.
insights.speaker: Maarten Lambrechts, Jonas Helth Lønborg, and Nishant Yonzan
insights.so_what: Headline evidence of long-run global convergence in living standards, useful context for EM growth and poverty-reduction theses.
insights.quote: the Global Prosperity Gap has halved from close to 13 in 1981 to 4.5 in 2026.
insights.locator: The Global Prosperity Gap has halved since the 1990s

insights.claim: East Asia and Pacific posted the most dramatic improvement of any region, moving from the largest Prosperity Gap (21) of any region in 1981 to the third-lowest (2.5) today.
insights.speaker: Maarten Lambrechts, Jonas Helth Lønborg, and Nishant Yonzan
insights.so_what: Marks East Asia and Pacific as the benchmark case of poverty reduction translating into broad-based prosperity, a comparison point for judging other developing regions' growth models.
insights.quote: having the largest (21) to the third lowest gap (2.5)
insights.locator: All regions have made significant progress since 1981

insights.claim: The Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan & Pakistan region and Sub-Saharan Africa have both stagnated in Prosperity Gap terms over the most recent decade, with Sub-Saharan Africa's current gap close to where the global average stood in the 1980s, putting the region roughly 40 years behind global progress.
insights.speaker: Maarten Lambrechts, Jonas Helth Lønborg, and Nishant Yonzan
entities: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan & Pakistan
insights.so_what: Identifies the two regions with the weakest near-term convergence trajectory, relevant for development-finance allocation and long-horizon frontier-market risk views.
insights.quote: the region is around 40 years behind global progress.
insights.locator: But the most recent decade has seen stagnation

top_entities: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan & Pakistan
